questions.optionB: Benzodiazepine overdose (competitive antagonist at GABA-A benzodiazepine binding site)
questions.explanation: Flumazenil: competitive antagonist at benzodiazepine binding site on GABA-A receptor → reverses sedation, respiratory depression. Used for: benzodiazepine overdose, reversal of procedural sedation. Caution: may precipitate seizures in chronic benzodiazepine users or mixed overdose (with seizure-causing drugs). Short half-life (re-sedation possible). Does NOT reverse barbiturate or alcohol effects. Naloxone: opioid antagonist. N-acetylcysteine: acetaminophen antidote. Reference: KD Tripathi — Flumazenil.

questions.questionText: Levodopa is the most effective drug for Parkinson's disease because it:
questions.optionB: Is a dopamine precursor that crosses the BBB (dopamine cannot) and is converted to dopamine in the brain
questions.explanation: L-DOPA: precursor of dopamine → crosses BBB via L-amino acid transporter → decarboxylated to dopamine by DOPA decarboxylase in brain. Dopamine itself cannot cross BBB. Given with carbidopa/benserazide (peripheral DOPA decarboxylase inhibitors — prevent peripheral conversion → reduce side effects, increase brain availability). Side effects: nausea, orthostatic hypotension, dyskinesias (long-term), 'on-off' phenomenon, wearing-off. Reference: KD Tripathi — Levodopa.

questions.questionText: Phenytoin is an antiepileptic drug that acts by:
questions.optionB: Blocking voltage-gated sodium channels (use-dependent/frequency-dependent block — stabilizes inactivated state)
questions.explanation: Phenytoin: blocks voltage-gated Na+ channels (preferentially binds to inactivated state → use-dependent block → more effective at high-frequency firing neurons). Uses: tonic-clonic, partial seizures (NOT absence). Zero-order kinetics at therapeutic doses (narrow TI — requires TDM). Side effects: gingival hyperplasia, hirsutism, acne, coarsening of facial features, diplopia, nystagmus, ataxia, megaloblastic anemia (folate interference), teratogenic (fetal hydantoin syndrome), osteomalacia (CYP450 inducer → vitamin D). Reference: KD Tripathi — Phenytoin.

questions.questionText: Naloxone is used as an antidote for:
questions.optionB: Opioid overdose (competitive antagonist at μ, κ, δ opioid receptors)
questions.explanation: Naloxone: pure opioid antagonist (μ, κ, δ receptors) → reverses respiratory depression, sedation, miosis from opioid overdose. IV/IM/SC/intranasal. Short half-life (~30-60 min) — may need repeated doses (especially for long-acting opioids like methadone). May precipitate acute withdrawal in opioid-dependent patients. Naltrexone: oral, longer-acting opioid antagonist — used for alcohol dependence and opioid relapse prevention. Reference: KD Tripathi — Naloxone.

questions.questionText: Lithium is used as a mood stabilizer in bipolar disorder and requires monitoring of:
questions.optionB: Serum lithium levels (narrow TI), thyroid function (hypothyroidism), and renal function (nephrogenic DI)
questions.explanation: Lithium: first-line for bipolar disorder (acute mania and maintenance). Narrow TI (0.6-1.2 mEq/L) → requires TDM. Toxicities: (1) Thyroid: hypothyroidism (inhibits T3/T4 release), goiter. (2) Renal: nephrogenic diabetes insipidus (downregulates AQP2 channels — polyuria, polydipsia), chronic interstitial nephritis. (3) CNS toxicity at high levels: tremor, ataxia, seizures, coma. (4) Cardiac: T-wave flattening. (5) Teratogenic: Ebstein's anomaly. Also: weight gain, acne, leukocytosis. Reference: KD Tripathi — Lithium.

questions.questionText: SSRIs (e.g., fluoxetine, sertraline) are first-line for depression because they:
questions.optionB: Selectively inhibit serotonin reuptake with fewer side effects than TCAs (no anticholinergic, no cardiotoxicity)
questions.explanation: SSRIs: selectively block serotonin (5-HT) reuptake transporter (SERT) → increased serotonin in synaptic cleft. Better safety profile than TCAs (no anticholinergic effects, no cardiac arrhythmias, safer in overdose). Side effects: GI (nausea, diarrhea), sexual dysfunction, insomnia/drowsiness, weight changes, serotonin syndrome (with MAOIs — hyperthermia, myoclonus, mental status changes). Fluoxetine: longest t½, CYP2D6 inhibitor. Onset: 2-4 weeks. Reference: KD Tripathi — SSRIs.

questions.questionText: Ketamine is a dissociative anesthetic that acts by:
questions.explanation: Ketamine: dissociative anesthetic — blocks NMDA (N-methyl-D-aspartate) glutamate receptor. Produces: dissociative analgesia, amnesia, sedation while maintaining airway reflexes and spontaneous respiration. Increases HR, BP (sympathomimetic). Used for: emergency/battlefield anesthesia, pediatric procedures, short procedures, chronic pain. Side effects: emergence phenomena (hallucinations, vivid dreams — prevented by benzodiazepines), increased ICP, nystagmus. S-ketamine (esketamine): FDA-approved for treatment-resistant depression. Reference: KD Tripathi — Ketamine.

questions.questionText: Haloperidol is a typical antipsychotic that acts by blocking:
questions.optionB: D2 dopamine receptors (in mesolimbic pathway → reduces positive symptoms of schizophrenia)
questions.explanation: Haloperidol: typical (first-generation) antipsychotic — potent D2 receptor blocker. Effective for positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions). EPS (extrapyramidal side effects — most common with typical): acute dystonia (treat: benztropine/diphenhydramine), akathisia, parkinsonism, tardive dyskinesia (late, may be irreversible). Also: neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS — fever, rigidity, altered consciousness, elevated CK — treat with dantrolene, bromocriptine). Atypicals (clozapine, risperidone, olanzapine): less EPS, block 5-HT2A + D2. Reference: KD Tripathi — Antipsychotics.
